_comment: Update this file when new exam papers are added. Map new themes to pillars in themeMapping. Add/update hook cards in hooks array. The Revision Tool loads this file dynamically at startup.

hooks.title: PMI design & benefit structure
bullets: Benefit: indemnity (reimburse actual cost) vs fixed-cash (set sum); indemnity passes medical inflation risk to insurer
Scope of cover: in-patient, day-patient, out-patient, diagnostics — each must be precisely defined
Sum insured restoration, reinstatement, NCB are India-specific features with material pricing and anti-selection implications
Co-payment and deductibles reduce moral hazard and premium but must be balanced against customer value
PED waiting period: standard 2-4 years; 48-month moratorium under IRDAI 2024 regulations; initial 30-day for non-accident
IRDAI mandates standard Arogya Sanjeevani; portability right at renewal; renewability guarantee — all affect pricing
Policy features to define: area of cover, second opinion, home nursing, organ transplant, AYUSH treatments

hooks.title: Income Protection design
bullets: IP pays a regular income benefit on inability to work; benefit = 50-75% of pre-disability earnings (capped to prevent over-insurance)
Deferred period (4, 13, 26, 52 weeks): longer deferred = lower premium; adverse selection risk
Benefit period: to retirement, to max age, or limited term; longer period increases longevity and investment risk
Own-occupation vs any-occupation definition: own-occ is more generous and expensive; any-occ harder to claim
Group IP: FCL avoids individual UW below limit; employer administers absences; rehabilitation reduces claim duration
Key pricing risks: incidence rate, recovery rate, claims duration, mortality of claimants, lapse behaviour

hooks.title: Critical Illness & LTC design
bullets: CI: lump sum on first diagnosis of specified condition(s); definitions must be precise and updated with medical practice
Core CI: cancer, heart attack, stroke; extended adds 30-50+ conditions; proliferation risk obscures pricing
LTC trigger: inability to perform 2-3 ADLs from 6 (washing, dressing, feeding, mobility, continence, toileting)
LTC benefit: indemnity vs cash; inflation-linking is critical for long policies
Pre-funded LTC vs immediate-needs annuity: different timing, pricing risk, and target market
CI reviewable premiums: reduces long-term pricing risk for insurer; creates repricing and lapse risk at review dates

hooks.title: Group scheme design
bullets: Group master policy: employer/association is policyholder; employees are insured lives; individual certificates issued
Free Cover Limit (FCL): lives below FCL require no individual UW; above FCL, individual evidence required
Employer-sponsored: employees may opt in/out creating adverse selection within scheme
Group IP: rehabilitation support; early intervention key; rate-at-risk vs pooled renewal depending on size
Mass government schemes: government as sponsor; political risk on premium; empanelled hospital fraud risk
PMJAY: Rs 5L per family per year; ~50 crore beneficiaries; tender-based pricing; experience monitoring critical

hooks.title: State healthcare & national systems
bullets: Healthcare system models: Beveridge (state-funded, UK), Bismarck (social insurance, Germany), out-of-pocket (India pre-PMJAY)
QALY (Quality Adjusted Life Year): HTA tool — one year of perfect health = 1 QALY; NICE threshold ~£30k/QALY
State provision defines floor for private demand; gaps drive product design and distribution strategy
India: low public spending (~1.3% GDP); OOP ~60% of total health spend; large private sector role
Risk equalisation: regulator redistributes risk between insurers; prevents adverse selection spiral under community rating

hooks.title: Unit-linked & hybrid products
bullets: Unit-linked H&C: benefit paid from investment units; insurer deducts morbidity charge periodically from unit fund
Unit reserve = value of unit fund at valuation date; non-unit reserve = PV future non-unit cash flows
New business strain under UL: initial expenses exceed early charges; recovered from future fund charges
Risk under UL: morbidity charge reviewable but TCF limits increases; unit fund value fluctuates with markets

hooks.title: New product development & market entry
bullets: Market assessment: size, demographics, competition, distribution readiness, regulatory environment, reinsurance availability
Greenfield vs acquisition: regulatory capital, time to profitability, brand, expertise, existing book risks
Appraisal value (M&A) = EV + value of future new business; synergies, run-off risks, integration cost
Product feasibility: data availability, reinsurance support, UW system capability, distribution partner readiness
IRDAI product filing: rate filing, benefit structure approval mandatory before launch; changes require re-filing

hooks.title: Micro-insurance & mass products
bullets: Designed for low-income: simple benefits, low premiums, minimal UW, basis risk accepted by policyholder
Distribution challenge: low income, low insurance literacy; requires intermediary (MFI, NGO, employer)
High lapse risk if premium burden is felt; government subsidy may be required to make viable
PMJAY: Rs 5L cover; cashless at empanelled hospitals; ~50 crore beneficiaries; state government co-contribution

hooks.title: Pricing methodology & assumptions
bullets: Claim cost = claim frequency × average claim severity; estimated separately; each requires IBNR-adjusted data
Burning cost = actual claims / exposure; must be on ultimate basis; trend to future pricing period
Key assumptions: morbidity rates, medical inflation, expenses, lapses, investment income, commission, capital cost
Medical inflation ~12-15% p.a. in India; applying historical trend forward is essential — failure leads to rapid deterioration
Profit loading = target ROE × required capital / premium; capital allocation method is a key modelling choice
Contingency loading: larger for new products, limited data, volatile lines; must be documented and justified
IRDAI rate filing: premium schedule filed before launch; rate changes require regulatory approval

hooks.title: Underwriting approaches
bullets: UW aims: classify risks into homogeneous groups; price commensurate with risk; decline only where risk cannot be quantified
Full Medical UW (FMU): complete medical history; clear policy position; reduces adverse selection; high admin cost
Moratorium UW: excludes conditions with symptoms/treatment in preceding 5 years; simpler; risk of claim disputes
Simplified/no UW: group schemes under FCL; small individual policies; wider access but higher adverse selection
UW ratings: standard / rated (extra premium) / excluded / declined; each requires documented rationale
AI-based UW (photo, wearables): faster; improves risk classification; raises regulatory fairness and data privacy concerns
Non-disclosure: if applicant withholds material fact, insurer may avoid or reduce benefit; burden of proof on insurer

hooks.title: Anti-selection & moral hazard
bullets: Anti-selection: higher-risk individuals more likely to purchase; pricing mix worse than general population assumed
Adverse selection spiral: healthy lives lapse when premiums rise; pool worsens; premiums rise further; repeat
Moral hazard: post-insurance behavioural change — over-utilisation, unnecessary procedures, delayed RTW
Controls: UW at entry, co-payments, deductibles, pre-authorisation, waiting periods, benefit limits
Anti-selective lapsing at renewal: premium increase drives healthy lives out; leaving sicker, higher-cost pool

hooks.title: Community vs risk rating
bullets: Community rating: all lives pay same premium regardless of age/health; cross-subsidy from low-risk to high-risk
Risk rating: individual premium reflects individual risk profile; actuarially fair but excludes high-risk from affordable cover
Community rating requires: open enrolment, minimum benefit standards, risk equalisation to prevent adverse selection spiral
Age-banding: intermediate approach — premium varies by age band not individual health; partial risk classification
IRDAI requirements contain community rating elements: renewability guarantee, portability, PED standardisation

hooks.title: Experience analysis & repricing
bullets: Compare A/E (actual vs expected) claims: frequency, severity, claim ratio; by age, sex, duration, cause, channel
Claim ratio = claims incurred / premiums earned; rising ratio with trend requires repricing or product change
Decompose deviation: volume effect (more claims), severity effect (higher cost per claim), mix effect (demographic shift)
Trend: project past experience forward to future pricing period; adjust for medical inflation, demographic aging, utilisation
Credibility: Z = n/(n+k); blend own vs industry data; Z near 1 if own data credible; k from variance analysis
Repricing decision: competitive position, lapse risk from premium increase, regulatory approval requirements

hooks.title: Renewal process & persistency
bullets: Rated renewal (individual): risk re-assessed; allows premium adjustment for changed health status at renewal
Pooled renewal (group): scheme repriced as whole based on scheme experience; no individual adjustment
NCD/NCB: reward claim-free years with SI increase or premium reduction; must be actuarially justified
Portability right (IRDAI): policyholder switches insurer at renewal; no fresh waiting periods for completed periods
Anti-selective lapsing: healthy lives leave if premiums rise too fast; worsens remaining pool — classic spiral trigger

hooks.title: Genetic testing & new data sources
bullets: Genetic results: most jurisdictions prohibit use as UW rating factor; creates asymmetric information risk for insurer
If insured knows poor genetic risk: adverse selection — loads up on CI/IP cover; insurer cannot detect or price
Wearables: real-time health metrics; reward healthy behaviour with premium discounts; privacy and data governance challenges
AI/photo-based UW: estimates age, BMI, health markers; faster processing; regulatory fairness concerns

hooks.title: Distribution & competition
bullets: Direct (internet/phone): low cost; limited product complexity; adverse selection risk if no UW at entry
Broker/IFA: advice for complex products; higher commission; access to affluent segment; TCF obligations on broker
Bancassurance: large customer base; cross-sell opportunity; limited H&C technical expertise; compliance risk
Group (employer): low UW, low unit cost, captive audience; high lapse on employment change
Commission-based: mis-selling risk; fee-based better aligned with TCF; IRDAI regulates commission levels

hooks.title: Claims control & management framework
bullets: Claims management objectives: fair and timely settlement; cost control; fraud prevention; accurate data for experience loop
Claims process: notification → assessment → authorisation → payment → monitoring → data capture; controls at each stage
Pre-authorisation: treatment approved by insurer before it occurs; most effective against over-treatment; requires TPA capacity
Cashless settlement: insurer settles directly with empanelled hospital; requires tariff agreements; popular India; improves NPS
TPA (Third Party Administrator): outsourced processing; improves speed; introduces operational, data quality, and oversight risk
Case management: complex/high-cost claims actively managed by specialist team; improves health outcome and controls cost
Claims data: ICD code (diagnosis), procedure code, hospital, insured demographics — feeds pricing, UW, and experience analysis

hooks.title: Fraud detection & prevention
bullets: Claim fraud types: ghost patients (non-existent), inflated bills, unnecessary procedures, duplicate claims, staged events
Provider-induced fraud: hospital/doctor inflates treatment for volume; harder to detect than policyholder fraud
Non-disclosure at application: withheld material facts; insurer may avoid policy at claim — burden of proof on insurer
Controls: pre-auth, random hospital audits, benchmarking actual vs expected cost per procedure/hospital, case management
Data analytics: flag outliers in claim patterns — high frequency, unusual procedure combinations, specific hospital clusters
Co-payment and deductibles: reduce moral hazard; make small-scale fraud economically unattractive

hooks.title: Medical inflation & cost containment
bullets: Medical inflation in India ~12-15% p.a.; driven by new technology, drug costs, hospital tariff increases, rising utilisation
Cost containment: preferred provider networks (PPNs) with negotiated tariffs; DRG-based fixed payments by diagnosis
Utilisation management: pre-auth, second medical opinion, disease management, discharge planning — reduces unnecessary treatment
Diagnostic-Related Groups (DRGs): fixed tariff per diagnosis regardless of actual length of stay — reduces over-treatment incentive
Volume discount: large insurer with high referral volume can negotiate better tariffs; competitive advantage

hooks.title: Cashless claims & network management
bullets: Cashless: insurer settles directly with empanelled hospital; policyholder pays co-pay only; improves customer experience
Empanelment criteria: NABH accreditation, tariff agreement, geographic coverage, quality standards, specialty breadth
TPA role in cashless: pre-auth approval, tariff negotiation, hospital audit, data capture; quality of TPA affects cost outcome
Non-network claims: reimbursement basis; allowed in emergencies; higher cost risk and fraud exposure
Network adequacy: geographic coverage including tier-2/3 cities; specialist care access; critical for customer satisfaction

hooks.title: IBNR & outstanding claims reserving
bullets: IBNR = Incurred But Not Reported; delay between treatment and claim notification; material for all H&C lines
IBNER = Incurred But Not Enough Reported; case reserves set too low on reported claims; development continues
PMI/CI: short IBNR tail (typically <3-6 months); IP disability claims can run years; LTC even longer
Methods: chain-ladder (link ratio development), Bornhuetter-Ferguson (blends actual with prior), Cape Cod; use data triangles
IBNR reserve uncertainty is a key audit focus; regulator monitors reserve adequacy via experience run-off analysis

hooks.title: Claim triggers & definitions
bullets: CI trigger: first diagnosis of specified condition meeting precise medical criteria; ambiguity is a major litigation risk
CI definitions must be updated as medical practice evolves; outdated definitions create disputes
IP trigger: own-occupation vs any-occupation; stepped definitions (own-occ for 2 years → any-occ) are common
LTC trigger: inability to perform 2+ ADLs from 6; objective third-party assessment is essential to reduce disputes
ADLs: washing, dressing, feeding, mobility, continence, toileting; cognitive impairment clause for dementia-related claims

hooks.title: Rehabilitation & return to work
bullets: Early intervention in IP claims reduces claim duration and total cost; vocational rehabilitation, occupational therapy
Return-to-work programme: graduated return, modified duties, employer-insurer coordination; benefit tapers to avoid poverty trap
Mental health claims: fastest growing IP category; specialist rehabilitation pathway required; stigma a barrier to early reporting
Rehabilitation spend is a claims cost but generates savings via shorter durations; net benefit must be demonstrated

hooks.title: Risk identification & classification
bullets: H&C risk taxonomy: morbidity, claims inflation, anti-selection, lapse, longevity (IP/LTC), expense, concentration, credit, operational
Morbidity risk: actual claims exceed expected; driven by incidence rate and/or claim severity deviations independently
Longevity risk (IP/LTC): claimants live longer than priced; claim payments extend beyond assumed duration
Concentration risk: geographic, employer-group, or provider concentration amplifies losses in adverse events (pandemic, recession)
Correlation in stress: recession simultaneously increases IP claims, lapses, and reduces investment income
Risk appetite: board-approved tolerance by risk type; drives reinsurance purchase, capital holding, UW strictness

hooks.title: Reinsurance structures & purposes
bullets: Proportional reinsurance: quota share (fixed % all risk) and surplus (above retention); insurer and reinsurer share premium and claims proportionally
Non-proportional: XL per risk (protects single large claim); stop-loss/aggregate XL (protects total claims above threshold)
Quota share: reduces capital strain; shares profit proportionally; useful for new business strain relief or small insurer
Risk premium arrangement: reinsurer takes pure morbidity risk; insurer retains expense and investment profit
Catastrophe/pandemic XL: protects against aggregate losses from single event; many treaties exclude pandemic post-COVID
Reinsurance also provides: technical support (pricing, claims, UW), badging (credibility for new entrant), capital relief
Reinsurer selection: financial strength (credit rating), technical expertise, treaty terms, pricing, claims handling track record

hooks.title: Capital requirements & solvency (India)
bullets: Available Solvency Margin (ASM): admissible assets − total liabilities; must be ≥ 150% of RSM at all times
Required Solvency Margin (RSM): maximum of (premium test: 20% net premiums, claims test: 26% net incurred claims)
Actions to improve solvency: new equity, sub-debt (within limits), financial reinsurance, expense reduction, repricing, portfolio run-off
IRDAI moving to Risk-Based Supervision; ICAAP/ORSA-style approach being introduced; more risk-sensitive than current factor-based
Capital vs reserving: both use same data but different bases; capital calculation uses net (after reinsurance) figures

hooks.title: Solvency II framework (SP1/SA1)
bullets: Three-pillar: Pillar 1 (quantitative requirements), Pillar 2 (governance/risk management), Pillar 3 (reporting/disclosure)
Pillar 1: SCR calibrated to 99.5% VaR over 1 year; MCR is lower bound (~25-45% of SCR); breach of MCR triggers supervisor action
Standard Formula (SF): prescribed factors by risk module; aggregated with correlations; conservative for complex portfolios
Internal Model (IM): requires regulatory approval; better reflects actual risk profile; significant model governance burden
Risk Margin: 6% CoC × Σ(SCR(t) × discount factor); declines as liabilities run off; can be material for LTC/IP
Pillar 2: ORSA, governance requirements, actuarial function, risk function; independent from each other

hooks.title: Stop-loss & XL layer pricing
bullets: Layer: insurer pays ground-up to attachment point; reinsurer pays between attachment and limit (exhaustion)
Burning cost of layer: expected claims in layer / exposure premium; requires full claim size distribution not just average
Claim size distribution: often Pareto or lognormal for large health claims; calibrated from historical data or reinsurer tables
Risk loading: function of standard deviation and skewness of loss in layer; increases as layer attaches higher
Aggregate stop-loss: triggers when total claims/year exceed threshold; pricing requires full distribution

hooks.title: Pandemic & catastrophe risk
bullets: Pandemic: simultaneous surge across products, geographies; non-diversifiable; cannot be reinsured cheaply post-COVID
COVID experience: IP claim surge; elective PMI claims deferred short-term; mental health spike; long-COVID creating new IBNR
Stress testing: model pandemic scenarios (mild/severe/extreme); assess capital adequacy; develop management actions pre-crisis
Many standard XL/stop-loss treaties now include pandemic exclusion clauses; specialist pandemic covers available but expensive

hooks.title: Financial reinsurance & capital management
bullets: Financial reinsurance: structured to provide capital relief or financing; risk transfer element must be genuine for regulatory recognition
New business strain relief: reinsurer advances capital to cover initial losses; repaid from future profits (experience refund)
Contingent capital: facility activated only on adverse experience; cheaper than permanent capital; basis risk if trigger not met
IRDAI permits financial reinsurance as solvency management tool for Indian insurers
Distinction from pure financing: must have genuine insurance risk transfer to qualify as reinsurance for regulatory purposes

hooks.title: Reserving & best estimate liabilities
bullets: Premium reserve: UPP (unearned premium) for unexpired risk + AURR (additional unexpired risk reserve) if expected loss-making
Claims reserve: case reserves (open reported) + IBNR + IBNER; use of development triangles; BF method blends actual with a priori
IP/LTC prospective reserve: PV(future claim payments) − PV(future premiums); requires seriatim calculation with recovery rates
Best estimate: unbiased, probability-weighted mean of discounted cash flows; no prudence margins; separate for Pillar 1
Valuation basis: prudent (MAD — Maximum Allowable Divergence) from best estimate; direction of prudence depends on risk type
Reserve adequacy tested via run-off: actual experience vs prior reserve estimate; deviations flagged to board and actuary

hooks.title: Assumption setting & data quality
bullets: Data quality dimensions: completeness, accuracy, consistency (across systems), timeliness; poor quality requires additional margins
Validation: reconcile policy count/premium to admin system; claims to accounts; exposure calculations; check for outliers/duplicates
Assumption hierarchy: own experience (if credible) > industry data > reinsurer experience > expert judgement; document rationale
Credibility: Z = n/(n+k); n = actual claims; k derived from variance; Z near 1 → own data dominant; near 0 → industry table
Best estimate: unbiased central estimate; explicitly separate from prudence margin added for valuation basis
Actuarial control cycle: assumptions → model → results → experience analysis → revised assumptions; board-reported

hooks.title: Embedded value & appraisal value
bullets: EV = Free Surplus + Required Capital + Value of In-Force (VIF)
VIF = PV of future distributable profits on in-force business after cost of required capital; discounted at risk discount rate
VNB = Value of New Business at point of sale; key management KPI for growth and profitability; sensitive to discount rate choice
MCEV (Market Consistent EV): values options and guarantees at market-consistent prices; more theoretically rigorous
TVOG (Time Value of Options and Guarantees): negative adjustment for embedded options in reviewable or participating products
EV movement analysis: expected unwind of discount + experience variances + assumption changes + VNB + capital flows
Appraisal value = EV + multiplier × VNB; used in M&A; multiplier reflects franchise value, growth prospects, and execution risk

hooks.title: Experience investigations
bullets: Purpose: update pricing/valuation assumptions; test model adequacy; identify adverse trends early for management action
Morbidity investigation: A/E incidence and severity by product, age, sex, duration, cause; deviations fed into pricing review
Recovery/termination investigation (IP/LTC): A/E recovery rates and claim durations; critical for prospective reserve adequacy
Lapse investigation: A/E persistency by policy year, channel, premium level, claims history; lapse assumptions affect VIF
Expense investigation: actual vs allocated expenses per policy, per claim, per NB case; identifies cost overruns

hooks.title: ALM & investment strategy
bullets: PMI liabilities: short duration (annual policy; claims settled <6 months); assets: liquid, short-term, high credit quality
IP/LTC liabilities: long duration (20-30 year disability claims); duration mismatch is a material risk; longer-dated bonds needed
Duration matching: match weighted average duration of assets to liabilities to immunise against interest rate movements
IRDAI investment regulations: prescribed minimum in government securities; limits on equity and alternative assets for GI companies
Matching Adjustment (Solvency II): applicable to annuity-like IP/LTC; requires matched bond portfolio; reduces BEL discount rate

hooks.title: Analysis of surplus & profit sources
bullets: Analysis of surplus: decomposes actual profit vs expected profit into component sources; management information tool
Morbidity surplus = expected claims − actual claims; positive = better than expected experience
Expense surplus = expense loadings − actual expenses; positive = cost better than assumed
Lapse surplus = impact of actual lapses vs expected; direction depends on whether product is lapse-supported or not
Investment surplus = actual investment income − assumed; sensitive to interest rate environment

hooks.title: Solvency II technical provisions (deep-dive)
bullets: BEL: probability-weighted discounted cash flows at risk-free rate; premium provision + claims provision calculated separately
Premium provision: future cash flows on bound but unearned business; can be negative if profitable (premium > future claims)
Claims provision: future payments on claims incurred (reported cases + IBNR + IBNER); discounted at risk-free
Risk Margin = 6% (CoC) × Σ(SCR(t) × DF(t)) over full liability run-off; projected SCR declines as liabilities run off
Volatility Adjustment (VA): adds spread component to risk-free rate; reduces BEL; sector/currency specific

hooks.title: IRDAI regulatory framework
bullets: IRDAI (Health Insurance) Regulations: standardise PED waiting periods (48-month moratorium under 2024 rules), renewability, exclusions
IRDAI (Insurance Products) Regulations 2024: new product classification; updated moratorium; clarified scope for standard products
Guidelines on Product Filing 2016: rate filing, benefit structure, policy wording; mandatory IRDAI approval before product launch
EOM Regulations 2016: caps on expense ratio by line of business; ceding commission treated as offset to EOM
IRDAI standardised products: Arogya Sanjeevani (indemnity), Arogya Rakshak (fixed benefit); must be offered before proprietary products
IRDAI annual return requirements: actuarial report, solvency certificate, experience statistics; Appointed Actuary must certify

hooks.title: Treating Customers Fairly & PPH
bullets: TCF principle: fair outcomes at every stage — product design, marketing, sales, servicing, claims, renewal, lapse
Board-level TCF policy required; outcomes monitored via complaints data, claims settlement rate, renewal rate, sales MI
IRDAI (Protection of Policyholders Interests) Regulations 2017: grievance resolution within 15 days; Ombudsman as escalation
Grievance redressal: internal process → Ombudsman → Consumer Forum → Court; IRDAI monitors insurer complaint ratios
Mental illness parity: IRDAI mandates equal treatment; health insurers cannot exclude mental health conditions

hooks.title: Appointed Actuary role (India)
bullets: Statutory role under IRDAI Act: must be Fellow IAI with practice certificate; independent from executive management line
Responsibilities: certify solvency margin (annually), review premium rates adequacy, sign actuarial report, board advisory
AA product filing: certifies premiums adequate and non-exploitative; cannot be overridden by management instruction
AA whistleblowing: if regulations not complied with, must report to IRDAI; cannot simply resign without disclosure
Professional standards: APS 33 (Appointed Actuary role), APS 34 (GI actuarial work); IAI mandatory; breach is disciplinary matter

hooks.title: PMJAY & mass government health schemes
bullets: PMJAY (Ayushman Bharat): Rs 5L per family per year; ~50 crore beneficiaries (bottom 40% of India population)
Cashless at ~25,000 empanelled government and private hospitals; benefit package defined by government
Pricing: tender process; private insurers bid on premium per family; government sets benefit scope — political risk on revision
Government as counterparty: risk that premium revision does not keep pace with medical inflation; no automatic adjustment clause
Fraud risk: empanelled hospitals have strong incentive to claim for procedures not performed; robust audit essential

hooks.title: Solvency & capital regulation (India)
bullets: RSM = max(premium test, claims test): 20% net premiums (first Rs 50 cr) + 10% excess vs 26% net incurred claims
ASM/RSM ≥ 150% at all times; breach triggers regulatory intervention; IRDAI may appoint administrator
Capital actions: rights issue, subordinated debt (within prescribed limits), financial reinsurance, dividend restriction
IRDAI moving to Risk-Based Supervision: more risk-sensitive capital framework; ORSA/ICAAP concept being piloted
Solvency certificate: issued by Appointed Actuary annually; regulatory filing; false certification is a serious offence

hooks.title: Taxation of health insurance (India)
bullets: Section 80D: tax deduction on health insurance premiums — ₹25,000 for self/family; ₹50,000 if policyholder is senior citizen
Additional ₹50,000 deduction if premium paid for senior citizen parents; total up to ₹1L deduction available
GST at 18% on health insurance premiums; significantly increases effective premium; government pressure to reduce to 5%
Premium tax relief incentivises purchase; removal or reduction would suppress demand — especially middle income segment

hooks.title: Professional guidance & ethics
bullets: IAI Code of Conduct: integrity, competence, confidentiality, professional behaviour; applies to all Fellows and Associates
APS (Actuarial Practice Standards): mandatory technical standards; non-compliance reportable to IAI Disciplinary Committee
Peer review: independent actuary reviews work before sign-off; reduces model error and judgement risk; required for key filings
Conflicts of interest: must be disclosed promptly; actuary cannot serve opposing parties simultaneously without consent
Expert judgement documentation: when data sparse, judgement must be documented with rationale, alternatives considered, sensitivity
